titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Roger Casement to Robert Monteith, 13 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Roger Casement

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter was sent by Sir Roger Casement to Robert Monteith about the visit of Fr. Crotty, a Dominican priest. Casement goes on to express concern for Monteith's welfare as Monteith left the previous night without saying goodbye and states that he will not rest easy until he sees Monteith again.Sir Roger Casement (1864-1916),was a humanitarian and Irish Nationalist. Casement believed that an Irish insurrection would be crushed unless it received substantial assistance from Germany. He spent eighteen months in Germany, arriving first as an envoy of Irish-American leaders, attempting to encourage Germany to support Irish separatist aspirations by providing arms. Casement succeeded in securing limited German support but his attempt to form a brigade of Irish soldiers in German prisoner of war camps to fight against Britain was largely unsuccessful. When it became clear that adequate help would not be forthcoming he travelled to Ireland by submarine. Casement landed and was arrested at Banna Strand, County Kerry on Good Friday 1916. He was tried in the Old Bailey for treason and subsequently executed by hanging at Pentonville Prison on 3 August 1916.Robert Monteith (1879-1956) was a founding member of the Irish Volunteers. In 1915 he was selected by Thomas Clarke (1858–1916), a veteran of revolutionary politics, to accompany Sir Roger Casement to Germany to assist with the recruitment of an 'Irish Brigade'.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Zossen Jan 13. 1916. Dear Mr Monteith, Fr. Crotty came &
has spent the day with
me. We went out to
the Camp together & he blessed
the men. Now he goes
in to Berlin to stay with
his Order (the Dominicans)
tonight & will return
to L. tomorrow evg.
They told me at the
Camp you were at the
Hotel S. so I send this there. I was alarmed about
you yesterday when the
waiter brought me the
cigarettes & said you had
gone. I ran out to
go with you & down to the station - I am so grieved
for you, and shall not
be easy till I see you here
again - and I beg you
to drop me from your
thoughts. The best way
to help me is to do
nothing perhaps - but come
back & with the the help of
God get the men straight
and off to Exercise & I hope on to the front. If only that were sure
& certain & I knew their business
was going to prosper I'd have
a millstone - one - off my
neck. But I want to feel
Easier about you yourself -
I am grieving for you & hoping to
see you soon - very soon. Fr. Crotty's Order has their House up
in the Moabite. Yours Sincerely, Roger Casement .
